Hey, welcome back here on our last two lessons of straight line prospecting. Rule number nine, always end with a powerful transition. And let me tell you exactly what I mean by that. So when you're done prospecting, when you've asked all the questions, you've exhaust your lines. You've done it logically, used the right tonality, the right by languages you respond, right? It's all perfect. You know what you need to know and you've gotten their pain points. What do you do? You need some simple, quick, elegant way to essentially lead them from you asking questions, finding out where their pain lies to you now the expert offering the solution. So you go from asking questions to finding out what their problem is to now offering this perfect tailor made solution that's going to fill their needs, resolve their pain and make them feel back in control again. And to do that, we use what's called a straight line transition. It's as simple as saying John, Jill, Mitch, Mia, based on everything you just said to me, the program I have or the product I have is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. That's it. Don't make up any other words. This just works. It's simple and essentially connects your prospecting to your presentation. I'll say it one more time. Jill, Jim, John, based on what you just said to me or based on everything you just said, this is a perfect fit for you or this is what I recommend. This is what you should be doing. Let me tell you exactly what. And then from there, you go into your straight line presentation. You get it? As simple as that. And remember here also, the transition also is the point where you're going to get rid of the look you lose and the mistakes or people who simply can't afford your product. Let's say that through your qualification, through your intelligence gathering, you found someone who really could use your product but they have no money. What would you say? Would you say, let me tell you about my product? No. You're wasting your time and theirs. It's not ethical. So now you're going to make the person say, I have this great solution but you can't afford it. Not very nice, right? So here's what you say. Jim, Jill, listen, the product I have is a perfect fit but honestly, it's really not within your budget right now. Here, maybe you can go to this place and point them in the right direction. Let them know where they can get help. Or perhaps just time them to direct them into a different program or product. One they can afford and it still fills as many of their needs as possible. But don't look at your transition as I'm going to take everyone down the straight line. That's what novices do. People who are experts in sales in the straight line system or any system seriously and they really all are on the straight line where they know it or not they're succeeding because it's just basic sales principles wrapped up into a perfect cohesive system, right? They're all saying to the look or lose, based on what I said, it's not for you. Have a great day. You're now out of the system. Now, you want to do it nicely and respectfully but you do not want to take everyone down the straight line because you're wasting your time and also getting a lot of bad reads meaning you're saying, why are they saying no to me? I don't get that when because they simply were never going to buy from anyone because they were just kicking the tires. That's the story. And now last but not least, we have our final rule of prospect. There's a lot of information. Let me do this one quickly but it's an important one and it is, I want you to stay on the straight line. Here's what I mean. This is the spot typically when a sale that's been going well meaning you took control, you established yourself the right ways. An expert sharp on the ball, enthusiastic, you got control. You then use that control to gather intelligence and as you gather that intelligence, you did it in a way using your active listening, your bilingual, your tonality to maintain and then build massive rapport on both a conscious and an unconscious level. But what happens sometimes is that if you don't know the finer points of the straight line system, this is where a salesperson tends to go flying off to Pluto or your anus which is not a very good place to be for salespeople, at least most of us. Point is this. What happens in practice is as follows. You ask someone a question and they will give you an answer that is germane to the situation. But then they start spiraling off to Pluto. In other words, let's say you ask them, so let me ask you this John, how long you're living in the area for? And they say, I've been living there for about three years and before I was living up in Northern England, all good so far, right? And then they start saying, yeah, I moved down there because I love duck hunting and the duck hunting isn't very good up there and it's much better down here. And next thing you know, they're on a whole soliloquy about duck hunting, right? That's spiraling off to Pluto. Here's my question. Do you number one, do you actually sit there and listen to them quietly? Let them go through their whole story without saying a single word. And then when they get to the end say, oh, wow, that's really great. And then you go on to your next question. So strategy number one is just listen. Strategy number two is you wait till they start spiraling off to Pluto and you say, whoa, whoa, whoa, buddy, stop. Do not spiral off here. You know, listen, we have to get back to the topic and hammer just the way you live. I'm not interested in those extra thoughts when you keep them on the straight line, right? You don't spiral off. Option three, you use this as an opportunity to build rapport by when they get to their part about duck hunting, you say, oh my God, you like duck hunting? I love duck hunting too. And next thing you know, you guys get into a conversation about duck hunting and you're saying, now that gets me to type rapport because we have that in common. Do you do that? Or lastly, do you let them talk about duck hunting and use your active listening skills to say, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, to show interest. And then when they're done spitting out, you then take them back to the straight line by moving on to the next question saying, oh, that's really great. Wow, that's really awesome. And how long you're living in the new place for? Which one of those do you do? There's four choices. Is it the first, second, third, or fourth? Pick one now. All right, so you think that it's number one. You basically get to sit there quietly and listen to someone ramble, right? And then just go on to the next one. Incorrect. The problem with that is that there is so much rapport lost when you remain silent. Remember your active listening. When someone is talking, whether the topic is germane to the sale or not, at any point, if they're going too long and there is no exchange of information in your part, no energy, uh-huh, yup, uh-huh, your rapport starts to do a nose dive. So you never can let that happen. Even when they're off the straight line, you always have to keep giving those uh-huhs, oops, and yups, okay, makes sense? And then once you have them and they spin out, that's when you then transition back into your next question. Okay, so you think you can actually cut them off and say, whoa, whoa, whoa, no duck-cunting talk around here, guys. I'm a straight line expert. You think that's going to go very well? Sorry, pal. You're wrong. If you do that, you know what? They will hate you. They'll say, oh yeah, I know this type. All you're she cares about is making a sale. Doesn't care a thing about me. So you can't do that. In fact, it's the very opposite. You want to let them keep talking and you're going to use your active listening to show interest. Yep, as they go, wow, and then when they're done, that's you say, oh wow, that sounds really good. You bring them back to the straight line and now you want the next question. All right, so that's a reasonable answer, right? So they talk about duck-cunting and you use this opportunity to say, oh my god, I love duck-cunting and you start talking about some slowing, some innocent ducks in Northern England, right? Not like four against duck-cunting, but that's your strategy because you show someone that since they like duck-cunting and you like duck-cunting, you guys are two pieces apart. Sorry, but your instincts are wildly incorrect. It's not your fault because it's counterintuitive, but what happens is this. Once you start going off into Pluto with them, they realize that you're not an expert. It's almost like an unconscious reaction saying, wow, this is really a really nice guy. He obviously has a lot of time on his hands because he's talking to me about duck-cunting right now. You're a professional life insurance salesman, not a duck-counter and you reveal yourself by for being a novice. And I've seen this happen so many times where the person will just get into such tight rapport, they think, and then after when they try to close, they're like, I don't get it. I thought the guy was so close. No, they weren't. They had tuned out a long time ago realizing intuitively, instinctively that anyone that spends that much time talking about a topic that's way off the topic is not very much of an expert. Now, they have a lot of extra time. They sound pretty desperate in fact. So that won't work. Instead, you want to use your active listening. So as they start spiraling out, you're like, uh-huh, show your interest like that. Oh, wow, great. And then when they're done, the spin out and say, wow, that's really awesome. Wow, as far as that, and then you go back to the next question and you'll be back on the straight line. Okay, well, guess what? You hit the nail right on the head. Boom, you got it. You see the beauty of the straight line is it allows you to really build rapport in the right way. In other words, when someone spirals out, that's okay people. They want to talk, they have a story to tell. And when you engage in that active listening, uh-huh, you show that you're interested, that you care. Not just not making money, but you also keep it tight enough in there. In other words, you don't let it spiral out, that you show that you're also someone, time is money. You're there to help them solve a certain problem and you calmly and using tonality to carry they, oh, wow, that's really great. Now as far as the, and you bring them back to the straight line and now you have earned their respect. That's the secret to effective prospect. And just so you know, I've tried this myself. I've tested this idea myself with the same story I told you about a doctor, my shoulder story. Well, this same doctor I taught was so great. I tried to take him to Pluto to ingratiate myself. And you know what he did? He's like, oh, that's really good you want to be right back to the straight line. Cause you know why? He was a true expert. Experts don't have time to ramble. They're not there to talk about the price of tea in China, politics in America, the fact that I can get them into the premiere of my movie. What surgeon is there to do what? Help me heal my pain. Now he's not going to blow me up and say, buddy, I don't give a shit about your personal life. He'll say, oh, very nice. Well, uh-huh. Oh great. Now how long has it hurt? And he stays on the straight line. I promise you, listen to me closely here, if you follow these rules of prospecting step by step and really master this stuff, when you edit your intelligence gathering session and you under those simple magic words will based on what you said to me, this is a perfect fit you. Let me tell you why and you lower your voice. You know what happens? They are like, what? I want to know. Wow. This guy's awesome. I, the secret for me when people say, how do you close such a high level? Well, of course I know the whole straight line, but if there's one thing that I think I really excel at is that by the time I'm done asking questions, they are so sure that I'm the one that's the person's going to help them get what they want, that they are hanging on my every word. And then of course I don't disappoint because I follow all the rules in the back end of the system from the sales presentation to looping. I keep that going, but that's the secret. There are no free words, no type of stupid statements, no going off the Pluto. And if you do this, you set yourself up for absolute greatness. Okay. And with that, that kind of wraps up our prospecting. One little bonus point here has to do with how you handle voicemails. Remember, as you're out there, you could run into a lot of answering machines and also if someone's on a cell phone, you'll hit their voicemail as well.
